questions.question: Prove that in a DFS tree, a non-root vertex u is an articulation point if and only if it has a child v such that low[v] >= disc[u].
keyPoints: Define disc and low formally
Show 'if': low[v] >= disc[u] means no back edge from v-subtree to ancestor of u, so removing u disconnects v-subtree
Show 'only if': If u is AP, removing u disconnects some child subtree; no path from that subtree to ancestors of u without going through u, so low[child] >= disc[u]
Perform DFS from vertex 1
Calculate disc and low values for all vertices
Apply articulation point conditions
Identify biconnected components from edge stack or articulation point separations

questions.question: Explain why the root of a DFS tree requires special treatment for articulation point detection. When is the root an articulation point?
keyPoints: Root has no ancestors, so low comparison doesn't apply
Root's low value equals disc[root]
Root disconnects graph if it has 2+ DFS children (multiple subtrees)
Example: star graph root has n-1 children

questions.question: Derive the time and space complexity of Tarjan's algorithm for finding biconnected components.
keyPoints: Single DFS traversal: O(V+E) time
Each vertex visited once, each edge examined twice (undirected)
Stack operations O(1) each
Space: O(V) for arrays + O(E) for edge stack worst case
Total O(V+E) space

questions.question: Prove that any two distinct biconnected components of a graph intersect in at most one vertex.
keyPoints: Assume two components share 2+ vertices
Show those vertices with edges form a cycle
Cycle makes union biconnected, contradicting maximality
Therefore intersection ≤ 1 vertex

questions.question: Differentiate between bridges and articulation points. Can a graph have bridges but no articulation points? Justify.
keyPoints: Bridge: edge whose removal disconnects graph
Articulation point: vertex whose removal disconnects graph
Yes - a cycle has bridges but no APs (no single edge disconnects a cycle)
Tree has both bridges and APs (except leaves)

questions.question: Given disc times from DFS: A=1, B=2, C=3, D=4, E=5, F=6 and low values: low[A]=1, low[B]=1, low[C]=3, low[D]=1, low[E]=5, low[F]=6. Identify all articulation points and bridges.
keyPoints: Non-root AP: check low[child] >= disc[parent]
For A (root): has multiple children? Check DFS tree structure
Bridge condition: low[child] > disc[parent]
A is root - need to know children from actual DFS tree structure

questions.question: Trace through Tarjan's algorithm step-by-step for the graph: vertices {A,B,C,D,E,F,G} with edges {A-B, B-C, C-A, C-D, D-E, E-F, F-D, D-G}. Show the DFS tree, disc times, low values, identify articulation points, bridges, and all biconnected components.
keyPoints: Perform DFS from A, show discovery order
Calculate low values bottom-up
Apply AP conditions for each vertex
Use edge stack to extract biconnected components
Show final components explicitly

questions.question: Write a recursive algorithm to find all articulation points in a graph using DFS. Prove its correctness and analyze its complexity.
keyPoints: Algorithm: initialize time=0, for each unvisited vertex call DFS(u, parent)
In DFS: disc[u]=low[u]=++time, count children
For each child v: if not visited, recursively call DFS, update low[u]=min(low[u], low[v]), check AP condition
If parent is null and children > 1, u is AP
Proof: based on articulation point theorem
Complexity: O(V+E) time, O(V) recursion stack

questions.question: Design an algorithm to find all biconnected components (blocks) of a graph. Your algorithm should use a stack to collect edges and output each component. Explain with an example graph containing at least 5 vertices and multiple biconnected components.
keyPoints: Use DFS with edge stack
Push edge (u,v) when traversing
When low[v] >= disc[u], pop edges to form component
Special handling for root
Example: graph with cycle + bridge + another cycle
